speaker: interviewer
text: With Betty Gold. This is reel number six.

speaker: interviewer
text: See these color bars here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to put those on the tape to begin with so that whenever anybody else looks at it, they can make sure that all the colors are lined up properly for whatever other tape, so that you don't come out having green skin or purple skin. That's why these colors are here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, some education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although I sort of think purple skin's sort of fun. But I'm pretty pink. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Well, then again, maybe purple skin's [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHTER] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, everyone hold still and be quiet, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture, taken in Cleveland, Ohio, in the United States, November 14, 1948. This wedding took care-- took place at the Heights Temple. And this was the first refugee to get married in America that I know of, certainly in Cleveland. And they came from far and near, from all over the country and the world to my wedding. I had about 700 people. It was standing room only. And they all came to see the monkey from the zoo, I guess, to see what I look like. [LAUGHS] And I felt-- when I walked down the aisle, I felt like a-- I was walking to-- like it was my funeral, because everybody was crying and weeping. And I don't know, it was very mixed emotions. I was upset why they were crying, and it made me nervous. But I was also happy to have all these people join me from far and near and join in our wonderful celebration. I can imagine what my parents felt like. And I was so proud that I gave him the [? Nachus, ?] that they lift Mary off their little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, the only thing you haven't told us is, who is this gentleman you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] I'm sorry. I married Morton Gold, my first boyfriend when-- that I met when I came to the United States. And he was born in Cleveland, Ohio, United States. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You told us about him when we were at the end of our tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was a great, great guy, and I'll forever be grateful in my heart to him for all the support, and confidence, and encouragement that he gave me. I think I am what I am because of him, because he really instilled it all in me, and whatever I accomplished, he must get the credit. He brought the best out of me. Morton died when he was 58 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I let go of the camera and do it, and it won't look good.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I sort of get one shot at this, so I want to make sure it looks good for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's one at a time, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I'm going to do each one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I-- can I say something about all three in the beginning, or no?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you'll stay longest on the oldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oldest is the bottom?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the top, right where you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, this is him?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Recognize him?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I thought it was the other way around. OK, let's-- we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess this is the reason I survived, my three sons. They're the loves of my life, and the life of my love. And I hope that, for generations to come, they'll be able to tell the story to their sons and grandsons, so on and on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love them very much. They're the most important thing in my life. And I guess God had a purpose for me, and here it is. This is my oldest son, Michael Gold. He was born in 1949, September 28. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What can I say about Michael?

speaker: interviewer
text: All you have to say is this is his bar mitzvah picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his bar mitzvah picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what we're going to say about all of them, OK?

words.text: to say about all of them,

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go to the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my middle son, Sheldon. He was born in 1954, December 18th. January 18, I'm sorry. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was named? Tell us, this one was named for your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this child is named after my oldest brother who perished in the Holocaust. And his name is Shimon. I named the second child after him because I was in denial when my first child was born, but I thought, in case I don't have any more children, I decided to face reality and name him after my beloved brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this his bar mitzvah picture too?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his bar mitzvah picture too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go to the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the baby of the family. This is Alan Gold, born 1956, December 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is his bar mitzvah picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his bar mitzvah. [LAUGHTER] This is also his bar mitzvah picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's here because we really wanted a girl, but I could never survive without him. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, there's one last thing we're going to do now, and that is we're going to photograph your whole family with you. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, I know that this is almost your whole family with you right now. You're missing one person. But if-- before we begin and I ask you any questions, I was wondering if you could introduce everyone to the camera, or to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll start with my oldest son, Michael Gold, his wife, Barbara Gold. My middle son, that's Sheldon Gold, and his wife, Lori Gold. And my youngest son, Alan, and his wife, Karen. They live in the  Clint. And this is my oldest grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, we're going to have to push in on this right now. Hold this down. Hold this so he can push in on this. And we'll see. OK. If you could kind of push-- no, you were holding it in the right place, but now-- this is hard. Turn it towards the camera. Just leave it there. That's good. That's good. We're just trying to get rid of the glare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tip it back just a little bit. Tip it forward a touch. Hold it right there. How's that look, Marla?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's great. Why don't you tell us who this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest son.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, this is not your oldest son.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] I'm sorry. This is my oldest grandson, the son of Michael and Barbara Gold. He's going-- he's 19 years old. He's attending Ohio State University, and I'm very sorry that he couldn't be with us. He's being missed a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Benjamin Gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin is 19 years old, and he's in second year of college.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now we're finished with this picture, we can pull back. And why don't you introduce the other two lovely young men who are sitting on each side of you? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my delightful grandson, David Gold, who belongs to my middle son, Sheldon, and his wife, Lori Gold. And this is my youngest grandson. His name is Evan Gold, who belongs to my middle son, Sheldon and Lori Gold. These are two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And David is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years old. And Evan is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five and a half.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five and a half years old. And they're wonderful, wonderful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. First, before I ask anyone anything, I know you talked-- you touched upon this at the end of our conversation earlier, but it's now 50 years since the war has ended. And I just wanted to know if there's anything you'd like to pass on to anyone from the experiences that you learned during the war about being a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I somehow have a feeling that the reason for my survival is continuity-- continuity of the Jewish people. And having had three sons, I hope that that managed to replace just a little speck of our Jewish people we lost. And I pray to God that all of them will marry Jewish, and reproduce Jewish, and marry Jewish, and so on and on and on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then that'll be a good reason for my life. I love them dearly. They're the most important thing in my life. What can I say? They make me very happy. And it wasn't easy, but they turned out to be very, very devoted sons and daughter-in-laws. And I have a wonderful family, and if nothing else, I mean, I've had it all. I've lived. I've accomplished. I couldn't be happier. Now I want to ask-- and you could speak in whichever order you want-- is there anything any of you would like to talk about? I want to wait until he focuses. OK. Michael, Sheldon, or Alan, about being second generation. Anything you feel that is important to discuss? This is an opportunity.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll start.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You want to push in on Alan, so he's going to be the first one. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Growing up, you learn a lot, going to junior high and high school, elementary school, and you realize that especially if you are Jewish, first generation-- first generation survivor, that things are a little bit different. You realize the type of house you grow up is a little bit different. And it also gives you a little appreciation for actually being here in the first place, because the chances of her surviving in the first place were very slim. And then for us to happen to make it here, you have a true appreciation for everything. And it's-- it's been fun. It's been exciting to have two brothers to grow up with. We've had us some really good times together. And-- but we've-- I've enjoyed it. It's been a good experience, and I would have it no other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, do you have anything? Let him pan over to you, and focus on you, too, at the same time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have some mixed emotions. I feel that, you know, I'm certainly glad my mother made it through it, but, you know, the stories, you know, the war stories, you know, we couldn't get much out of her when we were younger. But I'd get something from my uncle, I'd get something from my grandfather, I'd get a little bit from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, and everything put together, it's sort of you know, made me a little bit afraid growing up, actually. You know, what will happen in this world? Is there a God? How can there be a God when this happens? So I don't know. It-- you know, it-- It affected me in-- in-- in different ways, but-- but I have a family that I love, and that-- that turned out well. And-- and I have a son who I love and is doing well, and hopefully we'll go from there. When you said it affected you in different ways, do you want-- is there anything you would like to elaborate on about that, being a second generation? Is there anything that you feel you could talk about? Maybe that's a better way to phrase it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I think your mother wants to say something, but we'll let you go first. [LAUGHS] We'll let you go, Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know what you-- you know, what you want here for an answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't want an answer. I want you to say something that makes you feel comfortable. And if you don't have anything to say, then I don't want you to-- then don't feel obligated to say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess I just grew up, you know, less of a believer, you know, and more of-- you know, I try to believe in man more than a spiritual being. And I hope that the world would progress in a more positive manner as I was growing up. And as I was growing up, it didn't seem like the world was progressing in a more positive manner. [LAUGHS] But, you know, there's always hope for the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty wants to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my son--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on, Betty.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my sons were a little bit-- maybe it's unfair. They had to put up with a mom who had mixed emotions surviving the war, and maybe I took a lot out on them, or expected much more from them than any American mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always strived and tried to keep them Jewish, go to Hebrew school, remember who you are, remember where you're coming from, and all that. And that was a lot of pressure on my sons, and I don't know, I may say that-- you know, keeping kosher, I try to set the best examples for them so continuity can continue. But it wasn't easy. I realize it wasn't easy for them. You know, finish your plate. The kids are dying in India. And-- and you have to learn, go to Hebrew school. You have to survive. And what do-- you have to marry Jewish girls because I didn't survive to have a family and lose them again to the world. I-- this is my purpose. And-- so I really nudged them a lot. I nagged them to death. Now, maybe it was good, maybe it was bad. I don't know. So far, I don't regret it because it turned out that they-- they grew up to be very fine, respectable sons. They all married Jewish, gorgeous, wonderful, delicious young ladies. I'm so proud of them I could melt. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So maybe the nagging did work out. Maybe the investment I made, and maybe all the kidding that I took from them, and the ribbing, and I made mashed potatoes [INAUDIBLE] I mean, since they were born. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't offended. I wasn't insulted. I wasn't hurt. I understood. But I also felt I had a job to do. So far I think I did OK. And I'm very grateful to all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Shelly. Shelly is up there in the striped shirt. Do you have anything-- OK. Is there anything you want to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have the same feelings or thoughts that I think Mike and Alan had. I-- I didn't think of myself as a child of a survivor, or a child of a Jewish survivor, or Holocaust victim. I heard the stories. I didn't take them personal. I was very objective about those things. I had the same problems that Alan might have had, as far as living two cultures at one time. I was raised in one culture, and I-- and not culture of a survivor, but a culture of a person who was born in a different country, and who had different lifestyle, and came to this country and tried to carry on those traditions and those ways of life. Where in this-- in this country, the American culture was much different. And so I had a lot of not confusing-- I lived two cultures, basically, is what I did. But I didn't take it as a personal thing. The problems that my mother had, and the things she talked about, I believed was hers. It was her problem. I didn't take it personal. Sometimes she would even get upset with me because I didn't take this on as-- as my problem as well. But I-- I did notice it was a different in culture. But it was-- but it was OK. As a matter of fact, I-- it was-- it was-- it's like learning two languages at one time. It was something that was for a benefit.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you want to pull out. Don't worry. Now what I want to ask, if a, if either Evan or--

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: David have anything they'd like to say about their grandmother. And then I'd also like to ask if Lori, Barbara, or Karen has anything they'd like to also include. But you don't have to. You know, this is not-- we're not forcing you to say anything. I'm just-- if there's something you want to say. But if Evan or David have something they want to talk about. If they-- do you understand a little bit about what happened to your grandmother, either of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We both do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You do? Oh. Evan doesn't, really. But you do, David? You do a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you studied about the Holocaust a little bit in school or anything, or in Sunday school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have. And what do you think about what your grand-- can you talk about it at all? Can you think a little bit about maybe what your grandmother went through? Do you understand that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: You forgot. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. It's OK. It's OK. Do you-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] [LAUGHTER] Lori or Barbara or Karen, do you have anything that you would like to talk about? Barbara, you've been in the family the longest, so-- But I'm not-- I'm-- that was not like a-- making you say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. No, I have been in the family a long time, and I've always admired my mother in law. I've been hearing stories-- you know, little bits and pieces since I've been in the family, and just knowing where she came from it and what she went through, and-- you know, I've just always thought she was a strong woman and admire her for all that she's been through.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think that's really about it. If there's anything else anyone wants to say, this is your last chance. So get it out now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I can do it without crying. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK to cry, honey.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought, when Alan and I got married, I think there was a story that Betty had told us that she really wanted us to have the rabbi, you know, tell everyone that was at our wedding. And that was that, you know, there was Betty and Uncle Bernie that survived the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had three sons, I guess, in Bernie's family? And then three sons in Betty's family, and all of them had married Jewish girls. And Alan was the last one that was getting married. And so he completed this circle of, you know, marrying-- keeping within the religion. And that was-- that was real special to me. So I feel honored to be part of this family.

speaker: interviewee
text: She completed the circle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't do this without crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: So cry. That's OK, honey.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. If it's too difficult--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's actually a sweet story. When we came to Cleveland a couple years ago, we sold our house in Georgia, and we didn't have a home here in Cleveland yet. And we were in an apartment. And we were sort of-- we needed a place in a hurry to move to. And I told Betty that she should evict the people upstairs so that we could live here and be with her, and the kids would grow up with her. And she said, oh no, that wouldn't be a good relationship. And she helped us-- she helped me find a house, and she said that she didn't want us to be refugees the way she was, and that we should settle down and have a home here with our kids so we wouldn't have to move from home to home. And that was just the nicest thing that anybody's ever done.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [INAUDIBLE]. Thank you. I didn't realize I said, but I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think, unless anybody has anything else they want to say--

speaker: interviewee
text: David? Nobody?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you sure? Evan, do you want to say anything about your grandma? [INTERPOSING VOICES] OK. I want to thank you all-- Michael? I saw you open your mouth. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just going to say that, you know, it seemed like my mother's biggest wish was that we should all love each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that she got her wish, because we all love each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the most important thing for me. If there's one wish really I can-- I can have in my life and die peacefully is to know that my-- my children get along, and love each other, and protect each other, and be there for each other. That's-- that's the most important thing in my life. Family. This is my treasure. This is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I love them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Karen, do you think this would be the time to tell them all that [? Zeti ?] slipped me $10,000? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I owe you 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it.
